items.subject: history
items.topic: how the printing press changed the spread of scientific ideas
items.transcript: Before the fifteenth century, a scientific book existed only as a handwritten manuscript. Copying one by hand took a scribe months, and every copy introduced fresh errors: a misplaced number here, a garbled diagram there. So when Johannes Gutenberg introduced movable-type printing around 1450, the change wasn't just that books got cheaper. The deeper transformation was in how reliably knowledge could travel. Here's what I mean. Once a scientific text was set in type, every printed copy was identical. An astronomer in Krakow and one in Bologna could now argue over the exact same page, the exact same table of measurements. This is what historians call standardization, and it mattered enormously for science. Think about it. Science depends on other people being able to check your results. If two researchers can't even be sure they're reading the same data, progress stalls. Consider the case of anatomy. In 1543, Andreas Vesalius published a lavishly illustrated study of the human body. Because the woodcut illustrations printed cleanly and identically in every copy, students across Europe learned from the very same detailed images, rather than from crude, drifting hand-copies. Errors could now be spotted and corrected publicly, and corrections spread just as widely as the original text. The printing press also sped things up. An idea that once took a generation to circulate could now reach scholars in different countries within a year or two. That acceleration helped turn science from a scattered, local activity into a genuinely shared, cross-border enterprise. Next time, we'll look at how this same technology reshaped religious debate, because the printing press didn't only change laboratories. It changed churches, too.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How printing made scientific knowledge reliable and shareable
options.B: The life and career of Johannes Gutenberg
options.C: How medieval scribes copied manuscripts by hand
options.D: The role of anatomy in the history of medicine

questions.explanation: 教授全篇强调印刷术让科学文本'标准化'——每份副本完全一致、可被他人核对、传播更快，核心是知识变得可靠且可共享，故A正确。B只抓住古登堡这个人物，是以偏概全（too_narrow）。C手抄员只是开头用来做对比的背景细节，不是主旨（surface_word_trap）。D解剖学只是一个例子，且'医学史'不是本讲主题（wrong_topic）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what happened once a scientific text was set in type?

options.A: It made scientific books far more colorful and decorative
options.B: Every printed copy of the text was identical
options.C: It eliminated the need for scientists to check results
options.D: It allowed scribes to copy books more quickly

questions.explanation: 教授明确说'every printed copy was identical'（每份印刷副本都完全相同），故B正确。A把'插图印得清晰'夸大成'更花哨装饰'，改了事实（wrong_detail）。C与原文相反——教授说科学恰恰依赖他人核对结果，印刷是帮助核对而非取消核对（reversed_logic）。D印刷取代的是手抄，不是让手抄员抄得更快（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: Why does the professor discuss Vesalius's 1543 study of the human body?

options.A: To argue that anatomy was the most important science
options.B: To describe how woodcut illustrations were first invented
options.C: To give a concrete example of standardization's benefits
options.D: To explain that Vesalius illustrated his own book

questions.explanation: 维萨里的例子是用来具体说明'标准化'带来的好处：全欧学生看到完全相同的精细插图、错误能被公开纠正，故C正确。A把一个例子夸大成'解剖学最重要'，是过度推断（over_inference）。B教授没有讲木刻插图如何被发明，误认了目的（wrong_purpose）。D只是复述讲座里的字面内容（谁配了插图），没解释'为什么'提它（literal_restatement）。

questions.stem: What will the professor most likely discuss next?

options.A: How printing lowered the price of scientific books
options.B: How later inventions replaced the printing press
options.C: How printing slowed the spread of religious ideas
options.D: How the printing press affected religious debate

questions.explanation: 结尾预告：'Next time, we'll look at how this same technology reshaped religious debate'，故D正确。A是本讲已提到的细节（书更便宜），不是下节新话题（too_narrow）。B'后来的发明取代印刷术'与预告无关（wrong_topic）。C把'reshaped religious debate'反转为'减缓宗教思想传播'，方向相反（reversed_logic）。

items.subject: psychology
items.transcript: Imagine you spend a whole afternoon waiting in line for a restaurant, and the food turns out to be just okay. Do you admit you wasted your time? Usually not. Instead, you tell yourself the meal was actually pretty good. That little mental adjustment is at the heart of today's topic: cognitive dissonance. The term was introduced by psychologist Leon Festinger in the 1950s. Cognitive dissonance is the uncomfortable tension we feel when we hold two ideas that clash, or when our actions don't match our beliefs. And because that tension feels bad, we're motivated to reduce it. The interesting part is how we reduce it. Often it's easier to change our attitude than to change what we already did. Festinger ran a famous experiment to show this. He asked people to perform a boring task, then paid some of them one dollar and others twenty dollars to tell the next participant that the task was fun. Later, he asked everyone how they really felt. You might expect the people paid twenty dollars to like the task more. But it was the opposite. The people paid just one dollar rated the boring task as more enjoyable. Why? The twenty-dollar group had a solid reason for lying, namely the money. The one-dollar group did not. So to resolve the dissonance, they convinced themselves the task really was interesting. This tells us something important. When there is no easy external excuse for our behavior, we often change our beliefs to fit what we have done. Attitudes, it turns out, don't just drive our actions. Sometimes our actions quietly reshape our attitudes.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why people enjoy waiting in line at restaurants
options.B: How mental discomfort can lead people to change their attitudes
options.C: How Leon Festinger designed psychology experiments
options.D: Why paying people more improves their job performance

questions.explanation: 全讲围绕认知失调：内心紧张不适促使人改变态度以消除失调，故B正确。A把开头餐厅排队的例子误当主题，其实那只是引子（surface_word_trap）。C费斯汀格的实验只是证据之一，'他如何设计实验'不是主旨（too_narrow）。D报酬与工作表现的关系不是本讲内容，且被实验结果否定（wrong_topic）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is cognitive dissonance?

options.A: A reward given for completing a boring task
options.B: The pleasure people feel when beliefs and actions agree
options.C: The uncomfortable tension of holding two clashing ideas
options.D: A method for making boring tasks more enjoyable

questions.explanation: 教授定义：认知失调是'当我们持有两个相互冲突的想法、或行为与信念不一致时感到的不适紧张'，故C正确。A把实验里的报酬当成定义，张冠李戴（wrong_detail）。B说成'信念与行为一致时的愉悦'，与'冲突时的不适'完全相反（reversed_logic）。D把失调本身说成'让无聊任务变有趣的方法'，是相关但错位的表述（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Festinger's experiment find?

options.A: People paid twenty dollars enjoyed the task the most
options.B: Everyone rated the boring task as equally dull
options.C: Higher pay made people work harder on the task
options.D: People paid one dollar found the task more enjoyable

questions.explanation: 实验结果是只拿一美元的人反而给无聊任务更高的评价，故D正确。A正好相反，教授说结果与'拿二十美元更喜欢'的预期相反（reversed_logic）。B'所有人评价一样无聊'与两组差异明显的结论矛盾（wrong_detail）。C报酬影响的是态度评价而非'更努力工作'，实验没测工作努力度（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about attitudes?

options.A: Our actions can sometimes shape our attitudes
options.B: Attitudes always determine how people will behave
options.C: People can never change their deeply held beliefs
options.D: Higher payment usually produces more honest opinions

questions.explanation: 结尾点明'有时是我们的行为悄悄重塑了态度'，故A正确。B说'态度总是决定行为'，与'行为反过来塑造态度'的方向相反（reversed_logic）。C'人永远无法改变根深蒂固的信念'把结论推向绝对，过度概括（over_generalization）。D'钱给得多意见更诚实'恰被实验否定，是相关但不成立的推断（topic_adjacent）。

items.subject: cognitive_psychology
items.topic: the cocktail party effect and selective attention
items.transcript: You're at a crowded party, a dozen conversations happening at once, and yet you can follow the single voice of the person in front of you. Then, from across the room, someone says your name, and instantly your attention snaps toward it. This everyday ability has a name in psychology: the cocktail party effect. It describes our capacity to focus auditory attention on one source while filtering out competing sounds. The British scientist Colin Cherry studied this in the 1950s using what he called dichotic listening. He played two different spoken messages, one into each ear, and asked listeners to repeat aloud only the message in, say, the left ear. This repeating-aloud task is called shadowing. Cherry found that people were remarkably good at shadowing one ear while almost completely ignoring the other. When he later questioned them, they couldn't report the content of the unattended message. They did not know its meaning, and often did not even notice when it switched to a different language. Here's the puzzle, though. If the unattended ear is truly shut out, how do you still hear your own name coming from that side? That finding suggests the filtering isn't total. Some part of your mind must be processing the unattended channel, at least enough to detect something personally important. Psychologists took this to mean attention works less like a locked gate and more like a dimmer switch, turning down background information without switching it off entirely. So the cocktail party effect reveals a tension at the core of attention: we filter aggressively, yet the door is never fully closed.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: How people can improve their memory at social events
options.B: Why parties tend to be noisy and hard to enjoy
options.C: How we focus on one voice while filtering out other sounds
options.D: The history of British psychology in the 1950s

questions.explanation: 全讲解释鸡尾酒会效应——我们如何把听觉注意集中到一个声源、过滤其他声音，以及这种过滤并不彻底，故C正确。A'在社交场合改善记忆'不是本讲主题（wrong_topic）。B借用'party/noisy'关键词但方向错，讲座不谈派对为何吵闹（surface_word_trap）。D'1950年代英国心理学史'只是切利研究的时间背景，以偏概全（too_narrow）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is 'shadowing'?

options.A: Playing two different messages into the same ear
options.B: Detecting your own name in a noisy room
options.C: Ignoring one ear to rest the other completely
options.D: Repeating aloud the message from one ear

questions.explanation: 教授明确说'This repeating-aloud task is called shadowing'——大声跟读某只耳朵听到的信息，故D正确。A把两条信息'分别播入两耳'（dichotic listening）说成'播入同一只耳',改了事实（wrong_detail）。B听到自己名字是后文的谜题现象，不是shadowing的定义（topic_adjacent）。C'忽略一只耳让它休息'歪曲了任务目的，方向错（reversed_logic）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what did Cherry's listeners report about the unattended message?

options.A: They could not report its meaning or content
options.B: They repeated it more accurately than the other
options.C: They remembered it switching to a new language
options.D: They found it easier to follow than the shadowed one

questions.explanation: 教授说被试'couldn't report the content of the unattended message'，也不知其含义，故A正确。B与事实相反，他们跟读的是被注意那只耳朵，不是被忽略的（reversed_logic）。C原文说他们'often did not even notice when it switched to a different language'，即没察觉语言切换，C改成'记得切换'（wrong_detail）。D'比跟读的那条更容易跟上'讲座没提，也不合逻辑（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about how attention works?

options.A: It completely blocks out all unattended information
options.B: It reduces background information without fully removing it
options.C: It works the same for every sense, not just hearing
options.D: It depends mainly on how loud a sound is

questions.explanation: 教授用'调光开关'比喻：注意力是把背景信息调弱而非完全关闭（能听到自己名字就是证据），故B正确。A'完全屏蔽所有未被注意的信息'正被名字之谜否定，方向相反（reversed_logic）。C把听觉的结论推广到所有感官，过度概括（over_generalization）。D'主要取决于声音大小'讲座未支持，是相关但无据的推断（topic_adjacent）。

items.subject: physics
items.topic: how light refraction produces a rainbow
items.transcript: After a rain shower, when the sun comes back out, you sometimes see that familiar arc of color stretched across the sky. So where does a rainbow actually come from? It isn't an object sitting out there in space. It's what happens when sunlight passes through millions of tiny water droplets. Let me walk you through what goes on inside a single droplet. Sunlight looks white, but it's really a mixture of all colors. When a ray of that light enters a droplet, it slows down and bends. This bending of light as it moves from one material into another is called refraction. Here's the crucial detail: not every color bends by the same amount. Violet light bends the most, and red light bends the least. So the colors, which were traveling together, get spread apart, a process we call dispersion. Inside the droplet, the separated light then reflects off the back surface and refracts a second time as it exits. By the time it leaves, the single beam has fanned out into a band of distinct colors. Now, because red and violet exit each droplet at slightly different angles, your eye receives red light from higher droplets and violet light from lower ones. That's why the colors always appear in the same order, red on top and violet on the bottom. And here's something people find surprising. No two people ever see exactly the same rainbow, because the particular droplets sending light to your eyes depend on precisely where you're standing. A rainbow, in a real sense, is built just for you.

questions.stem: What is the lecture mainly about?

options.A: Why rainbows appear only after heavy rainstorms
options.B: How water droplets form in the atmosphere
options.C: Why the sun looks white instead of colored
options.D: How bending light inside droplets creates a rainbow

questions.explanation: 全讲解释光进入水滴时折射弯曲、不同颜色弯曲程度不同而分散，从而形成彩虹，故D正确。A'只在大暴雨后出现'借用雨的关键词，讲座没这么说（surface_word_trap）。B'水滴如何在大气中形成'不是本讲内容（wrong_topic）。C阳光为何看似白色只是中间的一句铺垫，非主旨（too_narrow）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, what is refraction?

options.A: The bending of light as it enters a new material
options.B: The reflection of light off a droplet's back surface
options.C: The splitting of white light into separate colors
options.D: The mixture of all colors into a single white beam

questions.explanation: 教授定义'This bending of light as it moves from one material into another is called refraction'，故A正确。B是光在水滴后壁的'反射'，是另一步骤，不是折射（wrong_detail）。C'白光分成不同颜色'是dispersion（色散），讲座用了不同术语，易混（topic_adjacent）。D方向相反——折射不是把颜色混成白光（reversed_logic）。

questions.stem: According to the professor, which color bends the most inside a droplet?

options.A: Red light bends the most of all the colors
options.B: Violet light bends more than the other colors
options.C: All colors bend by exactly the same amount
options.D: The color that bends most depends on the droplet's size

questions.explanation: 教授说'Violet light bends the most, and red light bends the least'，故B正确。A把红光说成弯曲最多，正好相反（reversed_logic）。C'所有颜色弯曲程度相同'与'不是每种颜色弯曲量都一样'直接矛盾（wrong_detail）。D'取决于水滴大小'讲座没提，是貌似合理的无据说法（topic_adjacent）。

questions.stem: What does the professor imply about a rainbow?

options.A: Everyone nearby sees exactly the same rainbow
options.B: A rainbow can be reached if you walk toward it
options.C: The rainbow you see is unique to your position
options.D: Rainbows always contain far more colors than we see

questions.explanation: 教授说没有两个人看到完全相同的彩虹，因为把光送进你眼睛的水滴取决于你站的位置，故C正确。A'附近每个人看到同一道彩虹'与'no two people see the same rainbow'相反（reversed_logic）。B'走过去就能到达彩虹'讲座没说，且彩虹非空间物体，属相关误推（topic_adjacent）。D'彩虹总含有远比我们看到的更多颜色'把内容夸大成绝对论断，过度概括（over_generalization）。
